title: OSC account setup
text:
1 Overview
During the workshop, we’ll be using the Ohio Supercomputer Center (OSC) so we can all run code and access data in the same manner. Additionally, OSC’s computing resources and data storage capabilities make it the go-to location for us at OSU to perform real-world omics data analyses.
The instructors will add you to the workshop’s OSC project (PAS3454), which will give you access to shared files and allow you to reserve computing resources. If you don’t yet have an OSC account, being added to the project will additionally trigger the process for you to create an account.

title: OSC account setup
text:
2 What you should do
When you receive an invitation email from OSC referencing the workshop’s project number PAS3454:

If you don’t have an OSC account yet
Follow the instructions and link in the email to create your OSC account
If you already have an OSC account
The email will likely tell you that you have been added to the project and don’t need to do anything

In either case, check whether you can log in
Go to https://ondemand.osc.edu and log in by typing your username and password on the left-hand side of the page. (If you just created your account, it can sometimes take up to half an hour or so before you can log in.)

title: Optional GitHub account setup
text:
1 Overview
For the purposes of this workshop, a GitHub account will allow you to use the agentic AI tool GitHub Copilot within VS Code.
The following steps are not strictly required before the workshop, because creating a GitHub account can be done quite seamlessly during the workshop, and because you’ll also have access to an alternative, similar AI tool (Claude Code).
However, we do recommend following the steps below. Claude Code is a paid tool that we will provide institutional1 access to during the workshop, but that you will not have access to after the workshop unless you pay for it in some way. GitHub Copilot, on the other hand, cannot be used institutionally at OSU, but does have a free ‘personal’ option for academic users. As such, for many of you, GitHub Copilot may be the easiest way to continue trying out agentic AI tools after the workshop (we’ll discuss your options further during the workshop).

title: Optional GitHub account setup
section: 2 Create a GitHub account (if you don’t have one yet)
text:
2 Create a GitHub account (if you don’t have one yet)
GitHub is a place where you can store your code and share it with others, and where you can find code and software written by others. It can therefore be very useful to have a GitHub account beyond GitHub Copilot usage.

Go to https://github.com
Click the “Sign Up” button all the way in the top right corner of the page
Fill out the form to provide your personal details (only use “Continue with Google” if you’re happy linking this to your Google account rather than your OSU email address – see below):

When choosing your username, best to keep it professional and include (parts of) your real name: you may use GitHub to share your code, like when publishing a paper
It’s recommended to sign up with your OSU email address, as that will make the educational access verification process smoother

Check your email and click the link to verify your email address. There may also be some two-factor authentication steps to complete.

title: Optional GitHub account setup
section: 3 Apply for free GitHub Copilot Pro access
text:
3 Apply for free GitHub Copilot Pro access
The following will work for teachers/faculty and students alike – you’ll just need to select the appropriate option in the form:

Go to https://github.com/settings/education/benefits
Click “Start an application”:







Fill out the form as needed. Note that you will most likely need a document that proves your current status at the university.







Application approval is often quite fast (sometimes in minutes), but access is only granted 72 hours after approval.

text:

title: VS Code installation
text:
1 Overview
In this workshop, we’ll use the Visual Studio Code (VS Code) editor for most hands-on tasks: to write and run code, to connect to OSC, and to deploy agentic AI tools. This Microsoft program is freely available for anyone and works on Windows, Mac, and Linux computers.
This brief page will help you to install VS Code on whatever laptop you plan to bring to the workshop.

title: VS Code installation
text:
2 What you should do

2.1 If you have an OSU-managed computer
If you are bringing an OSU computer, you may not have the required “administrative privileges” to install software on your computer the regular way.
However, your computer should then have an OSU application that allows you to install a selection of approved software, and that selection includes VS Code.

WindowsMac


The OSU app is called “Software Center”.
Details TBA


The OSU app is called “Ohio State Application Self Service”.

Open this application (it’s likely in the Dock, otherwise search for it)
In it, start typing “Visual Studio Code” in the little search box on the top left
Click the “Install” button that appears below the Visual Studio Code entry




This is the icon for the app on Jelmer’s Mac






If you start typing “Visual Studio Code” in the search box, this is what you should see: although this screenshot says “Update”; you should see “Install” unless you also have it installed aready.







2.2 If you have a personal computer
Installation is quite straightforward – follow the links below for the official VS Code installation instructions for:

Windows
Mac
Linux

title: Practical workshop information
text:
This is a 2-day, hands-on Ohio State workshop organized by the CFAES Bioinformatics Core and the Department of Plant Pathology. It is intended for researchers who want to learn how to use generative and agentic AI tools for omics data analysis and beyond.

Basic information

Dates: July 13 and 14, 2026, 9 am – 4 pm
Location: In-person in Columbus (Kottman 244) and Wooster (WSB 300) with Zoom connection
Instructors:

Menuka Bhandari (bioinformatician, CFAES Bioinformatics Core)
Jelmer Poelstra (lead, CFAES Bioinformatics Core)
Hannah Toth (postdoc, Department of Plant Pathology)
Jonathan Jacobs (professor, Department of Plant Pathology)

Detailed schedule: see the website’s front page
Attendance fee: $150 (an invoice will be sent after the workshop)



What you should bring to the workshop

A laptop – either OSU-managed or personal
Optional: A data set you’d like to analyze with AI tools during the workshop’s second afternoon (if you don’t have one, we can help you find and obtain a relevant publicly available data set during the workshop)







You don’t need to bring food or drinks, as these will be provided during the breaks.





What setup steps you should do before the workshop
As listed in the “Pre-workshop prep” dropdown in the top navigation bar of this website:

Install VS Code on your laptop
Create an OSC account if you don’t have one
Optional: Create a GitHub account and request free academic benefits



What you should know before the workshop
We expect you to have basic knowledge of the Ohio Supercomputer Center (OSC) and the Unix Shell (“terminal” / “command line”), including how to run simple shell scripts as Slurm jobs on OSC. Some knowledge of programming in R or Python is also helpful, but not required.
We will organize a 2-hour pre-workshop event for those who need to get up to speed on OSC and the Unix Shell, and will make its recording and other materials available for those who cannot attend.







The CFAES Bioinformatics Core also teaches the related 3-credit AU26 course “Practical Computing Skills for Omics Data” (PLNTPTH 5006). For more information, contact Jelmer Poelstra and see last year’s course website.







 Back to top

title: Our toolbox: intro and setup
text:
1 Overview
In the rest of this workshop, we’ll primarily use the following set of tools to enable us to run code, access data, and deploy agentic AI tools:

VS Code: a code editor that we’ll use to write and run code
GitHub Copilot and Claude Code, in the form of plugins for VS Code, for generative and agentic AI access
OSC: the Ohio Supercomputer Center, which will provide us with a powerful shared computing environment and access to data

This same setup is what the instructors use in their own work, and it is a setup that you can continue to use after the workshop (with some caveats regarding the AI tools, which we’ll discuss).

title: Our toolbox: intro and setup
text:
3 A first dive: GitHub Copilot
GitHub Copilot is an agentic AI tool that can help you write code and perform other tasks. Among other things, it is available as a pre-installed plugin for VS Code. While this is a Microsoft product, it is not one you can –currently– use institutionally at OSU. Instead, access runs through a personal GitHub account, which you can create for free and which you can use for other purposes as well.
When you open VS Code, you should see XXXX login button to log into GitHub. For those without a GitHub account, you can create one on the fly, including semi-automatically via a Google login if you have a Google account.



From https://github.blog/changelog/2025-07-15-social-login-with-google-is-now-generally-available


Unless you had previously set up GitHub Pro access, you will now be automatically using the Free Tier of GitHub Copilot. This free tier is not sufficient for sustained use of GitHub Copilot, but is sufficient for the purposes of this workshop, especially because you also have access to Claude Code (which we will set up later).

TBA - Question in the chat
TBA - Inline code example

title: Our toolbox: intro and setup

4 A brief overview of OSC

title: Our toolbox: intro and setup
text:
5 Connect VS Code to OSC
Next, we will set up VS Code to connect to OSC. This one-time setup is a bit of a process, but is well worth it. This so-called “SSH tunneling” of VS Code is a very convenient and powerful way to access OSC, with or without the added benefit of agentic AI tools. It will move a VS Code window entirely to OSC - not just the terminal, but also the file system, the code editor, and so on.

Open VS Code on your laptop
In the narrow sidebar on the left, click the square icon with four squares inside it (the “Extensions” icon), and now, the wide sidebar on the left will show a search box at the top and a list of extensions below it
In the search box, type “Remote Development” and you should see the “Remote Development extension pack” appear
Click the “Install” button to install this extension pack

Now, we will use functionality from this extension pack to connect to OSC:

Open the VS Code Command Palette by pressing Ctrl/⌘+Shift+P) and start typing “Remote SSH”:

Among the options that pop up, select “Remote-SSH: Add New SSH Host…” and type the following, replacing <osc-username> with your OSC username:
ssh <osc-username>@cardinal.osc.edu
For example, if your username is marie, you would type:
ssh marie@cardinal.osc.edu 
In the dialog “Select SSH configuration file to update” that pops up, select the first (top) option

You’ll see a “Host Added!” pop-up (may be in the bottom-right of your screen): in that pop-up, click “Connect”, and:

When you get a message like that shown below, click “Continue”:

“cardinal.osc.edu” has fingerprint <…>
Are you sure you want to continue?

Enter your OSC password when you’re prompted for it
If you’re asked about the operating system of the host, select Linux

Now, you’re connected, most likely in a new VS Code window, and some more pop-ups may appear:

If you’re asked whether you “Trust the Authors” of the folder you ended up in, click “Yes”
If a pop-up in the bottom-right asks you to update Git, click “No” and check “Don’t show again”

Open a folder
Close the window?







TipConnecting from now on



With the above one-time setup done, connecting to OSC with your local VS Code installation will be quick:

Open the Command Palette and start typing “SSH Connect”
Click on “SSH: Connect to Host” and then select your OSC connection
Enter your password (but this won’t be necessary anymore after you do the below setup!)

Also, any previously opened folders on OSC will….TBA








NoteOptional setup so you won’t have to enter your OSC password again (Click to expand)





If you take the following steps, you will no longer be prompted for your OSC password every time you connect to OSC with the above method (or with any other SSH-based method).

On your own computer, open a terminal application.

On a Mac, you should have an app called “Terminal” and potentially other options like “iTerm”: any of these fill work
On Windows 11+, this should be called the “Windows Terminal”

Generate a public-private SSH key-pair:
ssh-keygen -t rsa
You’ll get some output and will then be asked several questions, but in each case, you can just press Enter to select the default answer.
Transfer the public key to OSC’s Cardinal cluster:

WindowsMac


cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | ssh <user>@cardinal.osc.edu 'mkdir -p .ssh && cat >> .ssh/authorized_keys'


# Replace <user> by your username, e.g. "ssh-copy-id jelmer@cardinal.osc.edu"
ssh-copy-id <user>@cardinal.osc.edu



All done! Test if it works by connecting

In the Extensions sidebar of VS Code, search for “Claude Code” and install the extension “Claude Code for VS Code”
